screens.heading: A tradable IOU
screens.body: A bond is borrowing turned into a security. An issuer — a government or a company — borrows from investors and puts two promises in writing: regular interest payments along the way, and the face amount back at the end. What makes it a bond rather than just a loan is that the promise is standardized and transferable: it can be sold on to someone else, and the market puts a price on it every day.

screens.heading: Three numbers in the contract, one from the market
screens.body: The contract fixes the face value (the amount repaid at the end, and the base the interest is computed on), the coupon rate (the contractual interest, typically paid twice a year in the US), and the maturity (the date the debt ends). The fourth number — the price — is not in the contract at all. It is whatever the market will pay today, quoted per 100 of face value.

screens.heading: One bond, in numbers
screens.body: A company issues a bond with $1,000 face value, a 5% coupon, and a 10-year maturity. As the holder, you receive $50 a year (usually as two $25 payments) for ten years, then the $1,000 face amount at the end — provided the issuer keeps paying. That schedule is fixed the day the bond is born, no matter what markets do afterward.

screens.heading: A direct loan vs. a bond

left.label: Direct (bilateral) loan
left.body: Privately negotiated between a borrower and one lender or a small club. Terms are bespoke, the lender monitors the borrower directly, and there is usually no ready market to sell into — the lender generally expects to hold it.

right.label: Bond
right.body: A standardized security: uniform terms, many holders, and a secondary market that reprices it continuously. If you want out, you sell at whatever today's price is. Larger bonds are typically rated by public agencies, though not every bond carries a rating.

screens.verdict: The load-bearing difference is standardization and tradability, not the act of lending. The line is a spectrum, not a wall: syndicated corporate loans sit in between — distributed to many institutional lenders and actively traded in their own secondary market.

screens.heading: Why both sides show up
screens.body: Issuers borrow this way to raise large sums without giving up ownership — debt doesn't dilute shareholders the way issuing stock does. Investors lend for predictable income and for seniority: if things go wrong, debt holders stand ahead of stockholders in the queue. Insurers, pensions, and retirees are structurally hungry for exactly this trade.

screens.heading: "Fixed" income still moves
screens.body: The payments are fixed. The price is not. Between issue and maturity, a bond reprices continuously as interest rates and the issuer's creditworthiness change. Why and how it reprices is the entire next stretch of this path.
